coredata.dc:title: CuS-NPs, GQD, MSN-NPs and doxorubicin: An excellent nano-compound for cancer treatment by chemo-photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Gharibzadeh, Fatemeh
Shirkani, Hossein
Karimi, Sadegh
Mehrabi, Mohsen
Labkhandepoor, Elahe
coredata.dc:description: This study aims to create a nanocomposite that combines copper sulfide nanoparticles as a photosensitizer, graphene quantum dots as drug absorbers, mesoporous silica nanoparticles as a delivery mechanism, and doxorubicin, a traditional chemo dynamic drug used in cancer therapy. This novel nanocomposite aims to simultaneously utilize both photodynamic therapy and chemotherapy methods for cancer treatment. An 808 nm LED laser with 1 W power was used as a photodynamic therapy radiation source. Various techniques such as the transmission electron microscope, X-ray diffraction, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller/Barrett–Joyner–Halenda analysis, infrared Fourier spectroscopy, and UV–visible analysis were employed to determine the structural characteristics and optical properties of the synthesized nonocomposite. The sizes of graphene quantum dots, copper sulfide nanoparticles, and mesoporous silica nanoparticles were found to be approximately 40 nm, 8 nm, and 50 nm, respectively. The porosity analysis and specific surface area revealed that the optimized mesoporous silica nanoparticles have a high specific surface area of 717.76 m2 g−1, an appropriate pore size of 4.44 nm, and a well-defined pore volume of 0.99 cm3 g−1. These characteristics make them an up-and-coming agent for the delivery of anticancer drugs. The nanocomposite demonstrated a drug-loading capacity of 89%. Our findings showed a significant decrease in the absorption of anthracene composite after 10 min of laser irradiation, indicating the generation of reactive oxygen species.

head.ce:title: CuS-NPs, GQD, MSN-NPs and doxorubicin: An excellent nano-compound for cancer treatment by chemo-photodynamic therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to create a nanocomposite that combines copper sulfide nanoparticles as a photosensitizer, graphene quantum dots as drug absorbers, mesoporous silica nanoparticles as a delivery mechanism, and doxorubicin, a traditional chemo dynamic drug used in cancer therapy. This novel nanocomposite aims to simultaneously utilize both photodynamic therapy and chemotherapy methods for cancer treatment. An 808 nm LED laser with 1 W power was used as a photodynamic therapy radiation source. Various techniques such as the transmission electron microscope, X-ray diffraction, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller/Barrett–Joyner–Halenda analysis, infrared Fourier spectroscopy, and UV–visible analysis were employed to determine the structural characteristics and optical properties of the synthesized nonocomposite. The sizes of graphene quantum dots, copper sulfide nanoparticles, and mesoporous silica nanoparticles were found to be approximately 40 nm, 8 nm, and 50 nm, respectively. The porosity analysis and specific surface area revealed that the optimized mesoporous silica nanoparticles have a high specific surface area of 717.76 m2 g−1, an appropriate pore size of 4.44 nm, and a well-defined pore volume of 0.99 cm3 g−1. These characteristics make them an up-and-coming agent for the delivery of anticancer drugs. The nanocomposite demonstrated a drug-loading capacity of 89%. Our findings showed a significant decrease in the absorption of anthracene composite after 10 min of laser irradiation, indicating the generation of reactive oxygen species.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the three usual causes of death in both developed and developing countries. Nevertheless, conventional protocols in cancer treatments such as surgery, chemotherapy (CHT), and radiotherapy have significant drawbacks such as surgical wounds, drug resistance, and chronic symptoms [1,2]. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has recently gained attention as a cancer treatment approach due to its non-invasive nature and ability to minimize side effects. It offers advantages such as low systemic toxicity, minimal drug resistance, targeted drug accumulation in tumours, and the ability to concentrate light specifically on the tumour site, setting it apart from other treatment methods [2–5]. PDT involves injecting a non-toxic, light-sensitive drug into a cancer patient and then illuminating the tomour with light in the presence of oxygen, which produces toxic substances to destroy the cancer cells [1,4–7].

PDT has been a promising cancer treatment strategy since the early 1980 s [8]. It has received increasing attention after the discovery of photoferrin as a photodynamic agent in 1993 [9]. The effectiveness of PDT relies on the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which is influenced by several factors, including the type of photosensitizer (PS) material used, the intensity and wavelength of light, and the concentration of oxygen [4,7]. Commonly used photosensitive substances in PDT include porphyrin, phthalocyanine, and bacteriochlorin derivatives. Some photosensitizers have already been approved for clinical use [3,10]. However, these photosensitizers have certain limitations such as hydrophobicity, high dosage requirements, low selectivity, extended cellular absorption time, prolonged skin sensitivity, slow elimination from the body, tendency to accumulate in certain tissues, low stability under light, and limited absorption in the visible-ultraviolet regions [2,9,11]. PDT faces challenges related to the availability of suitable photosensitive materials, lack of penetration of visible light into tissues and the selection of appropriate light sources for complete and efficient treatment. Ideally, photosensitizers used in PDT should have excellent water dispersion properties, high light stability, and the ability to generate ROS [12]. Despite these challenges and limitations, there is a growing demand for more efficient and cost-effective options in PDT that possess characteristics such as high singlet oxygen production, good solubility in physiological conditions, optical stability, high selectivity for tumour cells, and biocompatibility. Advancements in these areas would significantly improve the effectiveness and practicality of PDT [2]. Therefore, it is reasonable to explore new methods that can mitigate undesired side effects while improving the treatment depth [4,5].

In recent years, copper mono-sulfide nanoparticles (CuS-NPs) have emerged as highly efficient photothermal agents due to their low cost, biodegradability, high photothermal conversion efficiency, and their simple synthesis route [13]. They have been widely used in cancer treatment [14–18]. Moreover, as mentioned, it was observed that CuS-NPs are also excellent candidates for PDT [19–24]. When excited by an 808 nm laser, CuS-NPs generate heat and ROS simultaneously, leading to significant anticancer effects in vitro and in vivo [23]. To address issues related to biocompatibility and drug loading capacity, associated with CuS-NPs, researchers have introduced mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN-NPs) to encapsulate CuS-NPs, creating a composite structure known as CuS/SiO2.

Silica has been designated as "generally recognized as safe" by the US Food and Drug Administration [25,26]. Mesoporous silica materials were initially reported by Kuroda et al. in Japan and further studied by scientists at Mobil oil in the United States during the 1990 s [27]. Silica exhibits superior biocompatibility compared to other metal oxides like titanium dioxide and iron, allowing it to effectively enter cells. The strong Si-O bond in silica eliminates the need for stabilizing additives commonly used in other delivery systems. Mesoporous materials have been extensively studied and proposed for use in cancer treatment, infectious disease treatment, and even the treatment of bone diseases [28–32]. The unique properties make them excellent candidates for drug delivery in cancer treatment.

Carbon nanomaterials have also shown great potential in bioimaging and biosensing due to their remarkable biocompatibility [9]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess exceptional attributes such as impressive photoluminescence, a large surface area, efficient carrier mobility, remarkable thermal and chemical stability, excellent water solubility, corrosion resistance, high optical and pH stability, and in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility. These GQDs are extensively used in various applications including biological imaging, biosensors, body labelling, drug delivery, and PDT, where they generate high levels of ROS [12].

For example, Zahedifar et al., utilized GQD in PDT along with white light in the range of 400–800 nm. This is due to GQD's high production of singlet oxygen, its environmental friendliness, stability under light and in different pH conditions. Additionally, GQD exhibited excellent dispersion properties in water and hydrophilicity, allowing it to disperse effectively within the body after exposure to radiation [33].
The exceptional efficiency of GQD in generating singlet oxygen can be attributed to a novel mechanism known as multi-mode sensitivity [2].

A significant recent breakthrough has been the development of GQD-based PDT agents that exhibit exceptional singlet oxygen generation, particularly in the visible light range. It is important to note that GQDs typically absorb light below 700 nm, resulting in limited light penetration of only a few millimeters [32,34,35]. This characteristic makes them well-suited for treating superficial tumours, such as those on the skin. However, it poses a challenge when treating deep-seated tumours in the head, neck, lung, prostate, or bladder [9]. NIR light, with its superior tissue penetration depth would serve as a more suitable illumination source than visible light. To the best of our knowledge, there have been no reports on using pure GQDs specifically for the infrared region. Furthermore, GQDs are widely recognized for their exceptional ability to adsorb drugs [36]. In this study, GQDs are employed as a versatile agent that serves a dual purpose: generating singlet oxygen and effectively adsorbing drugs. Moreover, our research has developed CuS/GQD/MSN (DOX) composites for the first time and explored their potential in cancer treatment by simultaneously utilizing PDT methods along with chemotherapy.

The required precursors for synthesizing the CuS/MSN/GQD nanocomposite were obtained from different sources. Glucose (C6H12O6), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, C19H42BrN), ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, SiC8H20O4), triethanolamine (TEA, C6H15NO3), copper chloride (CuCl2.3 H2O), and anthracene were purchased from the Merck Company. Sodium sulfide (Na2S .9 H2O) and ethanol were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Furthermore, for preparing the phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution, ortho-phosphoric acid (H3O4F) was sourced from the Merck Company, while potassium hydroxide (KOH) was obtained from Chem-Lab nv company. DI water and doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) were purchased from Kala-e-Thaitari and Pfizer respectively. All chemicals, reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The morphology and size of GQDs, MSN, CuS and their compositions were investigated using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (ZEISS Company, EM10C-100KV Model) and X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD). Diffraction patterns were recorded using an X-ray diffraction machine made by BRUKER, Germany, model Advance D8, with Kα-Cu radiation and a scanning angle of 10 ≤ 2θ≤ 80. The specific surface area, pore size and pore volume of the composites were measured using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analyses via the Asap2020 model (Micromeritics, USA). The infrared spectrum was measured using an FTIR spectrometer, model FT/IR 4600 (JASCO, a company made in Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was measured and recorded in a UV–visible spectrophotometer model 250 SPECORD made in Germany.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQD, CuS-NPs, MSN-NPs and Nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 depicts the schematic representation of the preparation steps involved in the preparation of the nanocomposite, as well as the underlying mechanism of its functionality.

To synthesize GQD, 0.1 gr of glucose was dissolved in 150 ml of deionized water at room temperature. The mixture was then transferred to a 500 ml stainless-steel autoclave with Teflon coating. Using a one-step hydrothermal method, the autoclave was placed in a furnace and heated to 200 °C for 8 h. After the hydrothermal treatment, the resulting solution was subjected to centrifugation at 14,000 rpm for 15 min to obtain a dispersed solution of GQDs. Fig. S1 illustrates the schematic representation of the steps involved in the synthesis of GQDs [37,38].

CuS nanoparticles were synthesized by adding 10 ml of CuCl2 solution (0.85 mg/ml) and 40 μL of CTAB (25 wt% solution) to 40 ml of water. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30 min. Afterwards, 50 μL of Na2S solution (60.54 mg/250 μL) was added and stirred for an additional 5 min. Subsequently, the mixture was then transferred to a water bath at 90 °C and kept for 15 min. Finally, the resulting mixture was rapidly cooled by placing it in cold water [39,40]. The steps for manufacturing CuS-NPs are illustrated in Fig. S2.

The synthesis of MSN was conducted following the established protocols previously developed by our research group [41]. The synthesis of MSN can be described in the following steps: (1) 2 g of hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and 665 μL of triethanolamine were added to 20 ml of deionized (DI) water. The mixture was then subjected to vigorous stirring and reflux conditions at a temperature of 95 °C. (2) Next, 1.5 ml of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), which serves as a silica source, should be added to the aqueous solution from step (1). The resulting mixture should be stirred for an additional hour under the same vigorous stirring and reflux conditions. (3) The mixture was allowed to cool down to room temperature, and subsequently underwent centrifugation at a speed of 10,000 rpm for a duration of 10 min. Then, it was washed twice with ethanol to remove unwanted residues. (4) Afterwards, 1 g of ammonium nitrate, in mild acidic condition, was dissolved in 45 ml of ethanol for template removal. This process was carried out under reflux conditions at a temperature of 60 °C for 45 min. Finally, the obtained nanoparticles were dried at 70 ℃ for 24 h. (Fig. S3).

In order to fabricate the CuS/GQDS/MSN nanocomposite, 2.5 gr of MSN and 0.5 ml of GQD were initially combined and stirred on a mixer. After 24 h, the synthesized CuS solution (10 ml) was introduced to the mixture and stirred on a stirrer for an additional 24 h. The resulting composite was obtained after two days and subsequently used for data analysis (refer to Fig. S4).

To determine the drug loading capacity, 1.1 mg of the nanocomposite was mixed with different concentrations of the doxorubicin drug (100, 250, 350, 500, 750, 1000 μL). The volume of all solutions was adjusted with buffered phosphate saline and the mixture was stirred under dark and sealed conditions for 24 h. Doxorubicin-loaded nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Quantitative analysis was performed by measuring the absorbance of the supernatant solution using a UV–visible instrument at a wavelength of 484 nm. This measurement allowed for the determination of the unloaded doxorubicin content. Finally, the drug loading capacity was determined by employing a standard calibration curve analysis [41] as outlined in Eq. 1 [26]:
(1)

To investigate the drug release of DOX from MSN-NPs, first 500 μL of DOX loaded in 1.1 mg of nanocomposite was suspended in 2 ml of PBS (1 M) in a dialysis membrane bag and the bag was immersed in 15 ml of PBS (pH 7.3 and 5.1). The released DOX was monitored by UV–vis at a wavelength of 484 nm at different time intervals over 48 h [41].

X-ray diffraction analysis was conducted to examine the structural characteristics of GQD, MSN, and CuS-NPs. The X-ray diffraction pattern of the synthesized materials is depicted in Fig. 2(a-d). Fig. 2a displays the X-ray diffraction pattern of GQD, revealing a broad peak at approximately 2θ= 22.5°, which corresponds to the (002) plates [2,12,33,37,42–46]. This peak corresponds to the International Center for Diffraction Data, Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) Card No. 75–0444 [37]. Moreover, the X-ray diffraction pattern of MSN exhibits a broad peak centered around 2θ= 23° (Fig. 2b), which confirms the amorphous and non-crystalline nature of MSN [47–49]. Fig. 2c illustrates the X-ray diffraction pattern of CuS-NPs. The observed diffraction pattern closely matches the standard diffraction pattern of CuS, specifically with card number JCPDS 96–900-0524. The peaks observed at 2θ = 27.35°, 29.27°, 31.67°, 32.53°, 48.01°, 52.74°, and 59.14° correspond to the crystal plates (101), (102), (103), (006), (110), (108), and (116), respectively. These peaks confirm the hexagonal structure of CuS-NPs, which is consistent with previous reports [50–52].

Fig. 2d displays the diffraction pattern of the synthesized CuS-NPs/GQD/MSN nanocomposite. The X-ray diffraction spectrum reveals the presence of all the characteristic peaks associated with CuS-NPs/GQD/MSN. This observation serves as solid evidence, confirming the successful fabrication of the synthesized nanocomposite.

The TEM images of CuS-NPs, GQD, MSN-NPs, and the nanocomposite are illustrated in Fig. 3(a-d). Fig. 3a reveals that the CuS-NPs were well dispersed in the aqueous solution, with a particle size mostly below 20 nm. Fig. 3b demonstrates the TEM image of the synthesized GQD obtained through the hydrothermal method. The image reveals the formation of nanoparticles with a nearly spherical shape and a relatively uniform size of around 30 nm. Additionally, Fig. 3c presents the TEM images of MSN-NPs. The images depict uniform spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of approximately 50 nm. The pores in the mesoporous structure are clearly visible throughout the particles. Fig. 3d exhibits the morphology of the CuS-NPs/GQD/MSN nanocomposite. In comparison to the MSN-NPs, the synthesized nanocomposite displays a larger diameter, which can be attributed to the coating of GQDs on the surface of the silica nanoparticles. Also, the mesoporous channels became unclear due to the introduction of CuS-NPs and graphene nanoparticles. In other words, the CuS-NPs and graphene nanoparticles are situated inside the pore and on the surface of the MSN, effectively filling the pore space.

The FTIR spectrum of CuS-NPs nanoparticles powder is depicted in Fig. 4a. The intense broad band observed in the 3200–3450 cm−1 range represents the stretching vibrations of the -OH group, can be attributed to both absorbed water and hydroxyl ions bound to the surface. The H-O-H bending vibration mode is indicated by the relatively intense band observed at 1637 cm−1. Finally, the band observed falls within the range of 500 to 600 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of the Cu-S bond, which is a characteristic feature of CuS-NPs[53–64]. Fig. 4b displays the FTIR spectrum of the GQD. The broad peak observed in the range of 3000 to 3500 cm−1 is associated with various O-H and N-H groups present in the GQD. Furthermore, the absorption bands at 2912 cm−1 and 1700 to 1760 cm−1 are assigned to the C-H and CO groups, respectively. The peak observed at 1614 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC bonds. The peak observed at 1285 cm−1 is associated with the O-H bending vibrations in the C-OH functional group, which enhances the hydrophilicity and stability of GQD in aqueous systems. Additionally, the peak around 1100 cm−1 signifies the stretching bond of C-O, as indicated in previous studies [37,38,42,65–68]. In Fig. 4c, the distinct peaks of MSN-NPs are depicted. The broad hydroxyl band observed at 2900–3700 cm−1 is due to the hydrolysis of ethyl orthosilicate [69]. Another peak located at 1641 cm−1 indicates the presence of absorbed water molecules through the -OH peak [70–72]. The two strong peaks at 1076 cm−1 and 798 cm−1 are associated with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si, respectively [69,70,73–76]. Additionally, two other peaks at 968 cm−1 and 455 cm−1 are related to Si-OH and Si-O-Si bending modes, respectively [41,69,71,73,74,76]. Fig. 4d illustrates the FTIR spectrum of the nanocomposite consisting of CuS-NPs/GQD/MSN-NPs. The prominent peaks corresponding to the characteristic properties of CuS-NPs, GQD, and MSN, as mentioned earlier, are evident in the spectrum. These distinct features confirm the presence of CuS-NPs, GQD, and MSN within the nanocomposite.

The absorption spectra of the samples were measured using UV–visible spectroscopy in the scanning range of 200–1100 nm. The UV–visible absorption spectrum ( Fig. 5a) of CuS-NPs exhibits a strong absorption peak in the UV and near-infrared region, specifically at around 1032 nm [40,77]. This ensures the significant potential of CuS-NPs for cancer treatment through phototherapy techniques like photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy, particularly utilizing 808 nm lasers. Moreover, in Fig. 5b, the UV–visible spectrum of GQD reveals a prominent peak at 230 nm. This peak corresponds to the π-π * transition of aromatic CC bonds in the sp2 hybridization region. Furthermore, there is an additional peak around 300 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π * transition of CO bonds, as described in reference [38]. Fig. 5c demonstrates that MSN exhibits two absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region, specifically at wavelengths around 213 nm and 251 nm. [78–80].

Finally, the UV-Vis spectrum of the synthesized nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 5d. As can be seen, a strong absorption in the ultraviolet region is attributed to the presence of CuS-NPs absorption peaks, GQD, and MSN in this region. Additionally, an absorption peak is observed in the near-infrared region at a wavelength of 1032 nm, due to the presence of CuS-NPs in the nanocomposite. This indicates that the synthesized material enables the utilization of two types of light sources, one in the ultraviolet region and the other in the near-infrared region, for cancer treatment using the PDT method. It is worth noting that since the use of ultraviolet rays is harmful to the body, a laser in the near-infrared region (808 nm) was employed in this research.

The BET/BJH analysis commonly used to determine the surface area, pore size, and volume of the samples. The basis of this method, which is based on the calculation of the absorption and desorption of gas such as nitrogen, is a fast and relatively cheap method, by analyzing its data, a statistical estimate of the surface size, the shape of the porosity, and the size of the micro, meso, and macro pores of the material can be obtained. In the case of GQDs, the analysis revealed a very low specific surface area of 28.09 m2 g−1. Based on these results, it can be concluded that GQDs are not considered porous samples.

As mentioned earlier, MSNs were used as drug carriers in this study. Surface area, pore size, and volume are three crucial parameters that significantly determine the drug loading capacity. To achieve the optimal surface area, pore size and volume of the nanoparticles we conducted a comparative study on two influential factors, temperature and time, during the template removal process. Based on the analysis of the results presented in Table 1, sample 2, which underwent a temperature of 60 degrees centigrade and a time of 45 min, was identified as the optimal choice and subsequently used in further experiments. The specific surface area of MSN samples was evaluated using nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms as shown in Fig. S5.a. The results show that the step-by-step absorption and desorption branch according to the classification of the IUPC is related to type IV isotherm, which indicates the presence of mesoporous nanomaterials. In fact, all the samples show a H1 residual loop with a small residual cycle at p/p° > 0.9 due to the capillary condensation of nitrogen into the mesopores. As shown in the figure, the isotherm nitrogen adsorption-desorption diagram of the obtained MSN-NPs (ammonium nitrate pattern extraction reflux), the closer distance between these two branches of the isotherm (absorption and desorption) is the uniform synthesis of NPs. This result is consistent with transmission electron microscope observations (Fig. 3c). In conclusion, the analysis conducted using the Brunaer-Emmett-Teller method indicated that the optimally synthesized MSNs exhibit a specific surface area of 717.76 m2.g−1. Additionally, calculations using the BJH method yielded a well-defined pore volume of 0.99 cm3.g−1 (Fig. S5.b). As can be seen in the Fig. S5.c&d, most of the pore sizes are around 3 nm, but the average pore size is reported to be 4.44 nm. These characteristics make these nanoparticles highly promising for anticancer drug delivery.

In photodynamic therapy (PDT), a photosensitizer (PS) is typically activated by absorbing light at a specific wavelength. This absorption converts the PS from its ground state to an excited state. Once in the excited state, the PS can undergo two mechanisms: type I and type II. The type I mechanism involves the transfer of a hydrogen atom in the form of free radicals. These free radicals then interact with molecular oxygen, converting it into oxygen radicals. In the type II mechanism, energy transfer occurs between the PS in its excited state and molecular oxygen in its ground state, known as triplet oxygen (3O2). This energy transfer results in the formation of singlet oxygen (1O2). Singlet oxygen has a very short lifespan and rapidly reacts with biological molecules, leading to damage in cancer cells[2]. Fig. 6 illustrates the schematic representation of the type I and type II mechanisms after photosensitizer excitation. Apart from their exceptional absorber, GQDs quantum dots act as photosensitizers that operate through the type II mechanism, leading to singlet oxygen generation.

In contrast to graphene, CuS is not considered a photosensitizer and does not possess a triplet state. Consequently, it does not generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) through energy transfer, unlike other photosensitizers. According to the literature [21,23], two potential pathways are identified for producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) in activated CuS nanoparticles when exposed to infrared light. The first mechanism involves heating effects, while the second involves the liberation of copper ions from CuS nanoparticles following heating in acidic tumour environments. These mechanisms are considered crucial for producing reactive oxygen species, which play a significant role in targeting and eradicating cancer cells. Reactive oxygen species can be likened to potent bullets that destroy cancer cells.

(1) One of the mechanisms, known as heating effects, involves the absorption of infrared energy. This absorption leads to the movement of charged particles and the rotation of water molecules, which increases temperature. Studies have reported that this temperature increase can cause the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and 8-oxo guanine. It has been observed that raising the temperature to 37–50 ℃ enhances the production of ROS. Furthermore, heat can activate dissolved oxygen, leading to the generation of various ROS, including singlet oxygen, superoxide radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and hydroxyl radicals [21,23].

Another mechanism, referred to as the release of copper ions, involves CuS nanoparticles releasing copper ions by lowering the pH level of the surrounding environment. This release of copper ions plays a significant role in generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) since copper ions can expedite the production of ROS. It is widely recognized in biological systems that the pH within tumour environments is typically lower (ranging from 3 to 5, and even lower in some instances [23] compared to normal tissue. Consequently, when CuS is introduced to tumours, some CuS nanoparticles can undergo decomposition, releasing copper ions. Copper ions, being a redox-active transition metal, exist in both monovalent (Cu+) and divalent (Cu2+) forms, which can enhance the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) within biological systems. This is because even trace amounts of dissolved copper can catalyze the conversion of the superoxide radical anion (O2
∙- ) conversion into the highly reactive hydroxyl radical (OH ∙ ) through a metal-catalyzed Haber-Weiss reaction. To facilitate the production of ROS, copper must undergo a cycle of oxidation and reduction, transitioning between the Cu+ and Cu2+ states. Additionally, free redox metal ions such as Cu2+ can generate hydroxyl radicals (OH ∙ ) by reacting with H2O2 through Haber-Weiss and Fenton reactions. These processes contribute to the acceleration of ROS production, which serves as a potent mechanism for targeting and destroying cancer cells based on the following equations [23]:

1

These results are consistent with our observation that singlet oxygen is one of the ROS generated when CuS nanoparticles are activated by infrared (IR) energy in tumour cells. This study uses the ROS generated through CuS-mediated near-infrared (NIR) heating for cancer therapy, as depicted in Fig. 7.

In PDT, a crucial aspect of investigation involves quantifying singlet oxygen levels. One of the distinctive features of singlet oxygen is its emission of light at a specific wavelength of 1250 nm. Due to their extremely short half-lives, directly detecting singlet oxygen molecules can be challenging on the order of microseconds. As a result, an alternative technique for detecting singlet oxygen involves the utilization of anthracene [8,12,33]. This study employed anthracene to indirectly detect singlet oxygen and measure the cytotoxic effects induced by nanoparticles. By examining the absorption spectrum of anthracene, singlet oxygen production can be indirectly confirmed. As singlet oxygen levels increase, the conversion of anthracene to anthraquinone will also increase, resulting in a decrease in anthracene's absorption or emission intensity. Briefly, 0.0004 gr of anthracene was dissolved in 30 ml of ethanol at 50 °C. Subsequently, 8 ml of the above solution was added to 3.5 ml of each investigated sample.

The obtained solution was divided into two groups: one without radiation and another subjected to radiation. A light source emitting at a wavelength of 808 nm for the samples treated with radiation was employed to induce the photodynamic effect. The absorbance of the solution at 357 nm was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. In contrast, the solution was exposed to NIR laser irradiation at a power density of 1 W for 10 min. Fig. 8 demonstrates that no decrease was observed in the absorption spectrum of anthracene before and after exposure to 808 nm laser irradiation. This finding confirms that the generation of singlet oxygen causes any observed decrease in absorption from the employed nanoparticles.

Fig. 9 and S6 illustrates a significant decrease in the absorption of anthracene when combined with CuS-NPs under 808 nm laser radiations for a duration of 10 min. This reduction can be attributed to the presence of singlet oxygen generated by the CuS-NPs.

Fig. 10 was used to investigate the potential ability of MSNs to generate singlet oxygen. Interestingly, no decrease in the absorption spectrum of anthracene was observed, indicating that these mesoporous nanoparticles do not produce singlet oxygen.

A slight decrease in the absorption spectrum of GQD was observed, suggesting that GQD has a limited ability to generate singlet oxygen in the near-infrared region. This finding is depicted in Fig. 11.

The photodynamic behaviour of a nanocomposite consisting of CuS-NPs, GQD, and MSN-NPs was examined using an anthracene probe. Two different concentrations of CuS-NPs were used to assess their impact on the photodynamic activity of the nanocomposite. After optimization, the experimental results showed a significant decrease in the absorption of anthracene when exposed to laser radiation, as shown in
Fig. 12 a and b. This reduction strongly indicates the generation of ROS during the photodynamic process. The findings confirm that the CuS-NPs/MSN-NPs/GQD nanocomposite can generate singlet oxygen when exposed to 808 nm laser irradiation. Significant singlet oxygen production highlights its suitability and potential as an effective PDT agent.
A diagram was extracted from the singlet oxygen production results related to anthracene's absorption peak at 357 nm.

As can be seen in Fig. 13, the most significant reduction is observed in the CuS group indicating that activating CuS-NPs produces more singlet oxygen than other groups. The CuS-NPs / GQD suffered the most significant decrease. The production of reactive OS related to composite CuS-NPs / MSN-NPs/GQD group experienced the second (group) was slower and less than that of free copper sulfide. This is due to the reduction of adequate energy absorbed by copper sulfide in the corresponding composite. No noticeable decrease in spectrum intensity was observed in the other groups.

By utilizing the standard curve of doxorubicin (refer to Fig. 14) and analyzing the visible absorption spectrum of the solution collected at 484 nm (refer to Fig. 15), the drug loading capacity in the nanocomposite was estimated to be approximately 89%. This value indicates a substantial amount of drug loaded within the nanocomposite.

Additionally, GQDs have shown a promising role in increasing drug loading efficiency. Our studies have revealed that incorporating GQDs into composites significantly enhances drug loading by up to 30% within the nanocomposite. This highlights the potential of graphene to improve drug adsorption and delivery within the nanocomposite system.

The results obtained from investigating the drug release are also shown in Fig. 16. As can be seen from this figure, in 48 h, the Dox release at pH = 7.3 is 25%, which increases to 75% when the pH changes to 5.1.

The present study aims to develop a composite material consisting of CuS-NPs, GQD, and MSN, which will be loaded with the drug DOX. The objective is to explore the potential of this composite in cancer treatment using chemo-photodynamic therapy approaches. Furthermore, the research also investigated the singlet oxygen production capabilities of each nonomaterial and their combined binary composition. CuS-NPs were chosen as the photosensitizer, GQD as a highly efficient drug absorber, MSNs-NPs as a delivery factor, and DOX as the drug of interest.

The analysis of structural specifications and optical properties of NPs and nano-compounds produced showed that all NPs were successfully made. The particle size of GQD was about 50 nm, CuS-NPs were about 8 nm, and MSN were about 40 nm. Additionally, two effective parameters, temperature and time, were examined to optimize the essential parameters of MSN-NPs. From the comparison of the obtained results, a temperature of 60 °C and a time of 45 min were chosen as the optimal temperature and time during the synthesis process. The results of the porosity and specific surface area of solids showed that the optimized MSNs have a high specific surface area of 717.76 m2 g−1 a suitable pore size of 4.44 nm, and a well-defined pore volume of 0.99 cm3 g−1, which has great potential for drug delivery.

In conclusion, the research determined that the nanocomposite exhibited a high drug-loading capacity of approximately 89%. The results indicated that combining CuS-NPs as a singlet oxygen production agent, MSN-NPs as a drug carrier, and GQD as a drug absorber within the nanocomposite showed promising potential for simultaneous use in photodynamic therapy and chemotherapy. These findings suggest that the developed nanocomposite could effectively achieve synergistic therapeutic outcomes.
